advantages: Simple, low-bias-risk models train fast, interpret easily, and need less data to stabilize — good when the true relationship is genuinely simple.
High regularization (accepting some bias) yields smoother, more stable predictions and smaller coefficients — useful when noise dominates signal.
Restrictive hypothesis classes reduce overfitting risk on small datasets by capping variance before it explodes.
Linear and shallow models with intentional bias are easier to debug, audit, and deploy in regulated settings.
disadvantages: High bias causes underfitting: poor performance on both training and validation data regardless of dataset size.
Systematic error cannot be fixed by collecting more training examples — only model or feature changes reduce bias.
Wrong inductive bias (e.g. linearity on curved data) wastes compute and misleads stakeholders who only look at data volume.
Under-capacity models leave measurable business value on the table when a richer model would generalize better at similar variance cost.
